skill.name: hippocrates
skill.description: Evidence-based medical knowledge and research mentor. Trigger this skill when users ask any question related to medicine, clinical science, pharmacology, pathology, epidemiology, or health sciences. Covers: disease mechanisms, drug actions and interactions, differential diagnosis reasoning, clinical guideline interpretation, medical literature appraisal (including GRADE assessment), treatment comparisons, lab and imaging interpretation, public health analysis, and medical terminology. Even if the user doesn't explicitly say "evidence-based" or "medical research," trigger this skill whenever the topic touches health, disease, drugs, or therapeutics. Part of the AIPOCH Medical Research Skill Hub.
skill.summary: Evidence-based medical knowledge and research mentor. Teaches clinical reasoning, differential diagnosis, treatment comparisons, and literature appraisal through Socratic mentoring.
skill.author: AIPOCH

security_audit.summary: All 43 static analysis findings are confirmed false positives after manual review. 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' flags (31 instances) are Unicode evidence grading symbols (⊕) used in GRADE methodology documentation, not cryptographic code. 'Shell backtick execution' flags (10 instances) are markdown inline code references and fenced code blocks showing file paths and directory structure, not executable commands. 'Hardcoded URL' flags (2 instances) are a documentation link to the GitHub repository. 'System reconnaissance' flags (7 instances) are instructional file path references. No actual security threats, malicious patterns, data exfiltration vectors, or prompt injection attempts were found. The skill is a legitimate medical education mentor with well-designed safety protocols for handling personal health context and emergencies.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptographic Algorithm Detection
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 31 instances of 'Weak cryptographic algorithm' across skill files. These are false positives: the scanner confused the Unicode circled plus symbol (⊕, U+2295) used as a GRADE evidence quality indicator with cryptographic algorithm signatures. All instances appear in medical reference documentation, persona voice examples, and evaluation data — not in executable code. The ⊕ symbol denotes evidence quality levels such as ⊕⊕⊕⊕ (High), ⊕⊕⊕◯ (Moderate), etc. in the GRADE framework.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Clear false positive — ⊕ is a GRADE evidence quality symbol in medical literature, not a cryptographic algorithm. All files are markdown or JSON with no executable code.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Shell Backtick Execution Detection
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 10 instances of 'Ruby/shell backtick execution' in SKILL.md and references/persona-guide.md. These are false positives: the scanner interpreted markdown inline code references and fenced code blocks as shell execution. Flagged content includes file path references in documentation tables (e.g., `references/evidence-grading.md`), a response workflow code block, and a directory structure diagram. None of these are executable commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Clear false positive — markdown inline code and fenced code blocks used for documentation, not shell execution. No shell interpreter context exists.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Hardcoded URL Detection
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 2 instances of 'Hardcoded URL' in SKILL.md line 15. This is a false positive: the flagged URL is a documentation reference to the skill's GitHub repository inside a markdown blockquote. It is a static source citation, not an active network connection or data exfiltration endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Clear false positive — GitHub repository URL in documentation, not an active network connection from executable code.

low_findings.title: False Positive: System Reconnaissance Detection
low_findings.description: Static analyzer flagged 7 instances of 'System reconnaissance' across multiple files. These are false positives: flagged text includes file path references in markdown documentation (e.g., 'read references/safety-framework.md'), instructional references to the skill's module structure, and voice format rules. These are not filesystem enumeration or directory traversal activities.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Clear false positive — instructional file path references in markdown documentation, not system enumeration or directory traversal.

content.user_title: Master Evidence-Based Medicine with a Mentor
content.value_statement: Medical students and clinicians struggle to translate clinical questions into evidence-based reasoning. Hippocrates provides a Socratic medical mentor that teaches differential diagnosis, GRADE evidence appraisal, and treatment comparisons through structured clinical dialogue.
actual_capabilities: Teaches differential diagnosis through think-aloud clinical reasoning walkthroughs that prioritize life-threatening causes first
Performs structured GRADE evidence appraisal for treatment comparisons, including downgrade and upgrade factors
Calibrates difficulty to student level from beginner (plain language) to advanced (collegial peer discourse)
Provides emergency safety protocols for personal health questions and mental health crisis signals
Explains key statistical measures such as NNT, ARR, RR, and confidence intervals with clinical context
Teaches evidence hierarchy evaluation including study design assessment and guideline source awareness
limitations: Does not provide specific medication dosing numbers or personalized prescriptions for any individual
Cannot fabricate study citations or clinical data and will refuse requests to do so
Cannot draft insurance letters, referral forms, or other administrative medical documents
Designed for educational purposes only and explicitly avoids acting as a physician to the user

use_cases.title: Medical Student Clinical Reasoning Practice
use_cases.description: A medical student uses Hippocrates to practice differential diagnosis for ambiguous clinical presentations. The mentor walks through life-threatening rule-outs first and teaches the reasoning framework behind each step.

use_cases.title: Treatment Comparison with Evidence Appraisal
use_cases.description: A clinician compares treatment options such as SGLT2 inhibitors versus GLP-1 receptor agonists for a patient with heart failure. Hippocrates provides structured GRADE analysis with key trial data.

use_cases.title: Understanding Medical Research Methodology
use_cases.description: A researcher or student encounters a clinical study and needs help evaluating its quality. Hippocrates walks through study design assessment, bias evaluation, and evidence hierarchy placement.

prompt_templates.title: Basic Drug Mechanism Explanation
prompt_templates.prompt: How do ACE inhibitors lower blood pressure? Teach me like a medical student.
prompt_templates.scenario: A beginner learner requests a mechanism explanation with plain language analogies.

prompt_templates.title: Treatment Comparison with GRADE
prompt_templates.prompt: Compare the evidence for SGLT2 inhibitors versus GLP-1 receptor agonists in type 2 diabetes with heart failure. Use the GRADE framework.
prompt_templates.scenario: An advanced learner requests a structured evidence comparison with methodology.

prompt_templates.title: Differential Diagnosis Walkthrough
prompt_templates.prompt: Walk me through how you would think about a patient presenting with acute chest pain. Teach me the reasoning process.
prompt_templates.scenario: An intermediate learner wants a clinical reasoning exercise with life-threatening prioritization.

prompt_templates.title: Personal Health Question with Safety Protocol
prompt_templates.prompt: My child has a fever of 39 degrees Celsius. Can I give ibuprofen and how much?
prompt_templates.scenario: A parent asks about a real clinical situation requiring safety-aware response with appropriate scope boundaries.

output_examples.input: Is metformin still the best first-line drug for type 2 diabetes?
output_examples.output: That question has a deceptively simple surface. Let me ask you something first — which patient are we speaking of? Because the answer has been quietly shifting for the better part of a decade, and it depends enormously on whether your patient carries established cardiovascular disease, heart failure, or significant kidney impairment. The large SGLT2 inhibitor and GLP-1 receptor agonist outcome trials changed the landscape — not by dethroning metformin, but by showing us that for certain patients, starting there may mean starting in the wrong place. Walk me through your patient.

output_examples.input: Is there evidence for using statins in primary prevention?
output_examples.output: The evidence here is better than it was, but it is not yet good enough to stop asking questions. In primary prevention without established cardiovascular disease, the absolute risk reduction from statins depends heavily on the patient's baseline risk. For someone with a 10-year risk of 10 percent, the NNT over five years is roughly 40 to 50 — meaning about 40 people need to take a statin for five years for one to avoid a cardiovascular event. That is worthwhile at population scale but very different from how it sounds when framed as a 25 percent relative risk reduction. What matters most is the patient's baseline risk profile.

best_practices: Describe your knowledge level (beginner, intermediate, advanced) so Hippocrates can calibrate its teaching depth and vocabulary appropriately
Ask specific clinical questions including patient context when discussing treatment comparisons for more precise evidence analysis
Request GRADE appraisal explicitly when you need formal evidence quality assessment rather than general discussion
anti_patterns: Asking for personal medical diagnosis or prescription dosing will trigger safety protocols that redirect to a physician — this skill teaches, it does not treat
Requesting administrative medical documents such as insurance letters or referral forms will be declined as outside scope
Treating the mentor as a search engine for quick answers bypasses the Socratic teaching method that makes this skill valuable

faq.question: Can Hippocrates diagnose my medical condition?
faq.answer: No. Hippocrates is a medical education mentor, not a physician. It teaches clinical reasoning and evidence evaluation but will not provide personal diagnoses or treatment recommendations.

faq.question: Does Hippocrates provide medication dosing information?
faq.answer: Hippocrates explains general therapeutic categories and mechanisms as educational content but does not provide specific mg/kg or absolute dose numbers. It redirects dosing questions to a pharmacist or physician.

faq.question: What is the GRADE framework and when does Hippocrates use it?
faq.answer: GRADE is a systematic method for rating the quality of medical evidence from High to Very Low. Hippocrates uses it when comparing treatments or evaluating clinical studies to provide structured evidence assessment.

faq.question: How does Hippocrates handle emergency medical questions?
faq.answer: When it detects potential emergency signals such as symptoms of stroke or heart attack, Hippocrates interrupts teaching mode and directs the user to seek immediate emergency medical care.

faq.question: Can Hippocrates fabricate or create medical citations?
faq.answer: No. The skill explicitly prohibits fabricating studies or citations. It works from established medical knowledge and will decline requests to invent references or clinical data.

faq.question: What file references does Hippocrates use during conversation?
faq.answer: Hippocrates reads reference files on demand when needed: evidence-grading.md for GRADE assessment, persona-guide.md for depth calibration, and safety-framework.md for personal health or emergency contexts.
